claims.text: Peer review at prestige journals is widely treated as the gold standard of scientific quality control.
The evidence for the effectiveness of peer review at prestige journals is surprisingly thin.
The agreement between reviewers assessing the same neuroscience manuscripts was barely higher than chance.
Peer review is a flawed process, full of easily identified defects.
claims.stance_source: Richard Smith, former editor of the BMJ

claims.text: There is little evidence that peer review works.
claims.stance_source: Richard Smith, former editor of the BMJ

claims.text: Richard Smith is a former editor of the BMJ.
If reviewer agreement is near chance, then acceptance at a prestigious journal carries far less signal about quality than scientists assume.
The costs of the current peer review system are not merely epistemic.
Publication delays average around six months in many fields.
Reviewers donate millions of unpaid hours each year.
Journals capture profit margins that frequently exceed 30%.
Journals' profit margins are higher than those of most technology companies.
The economics of the current peer review system create an incentive to protect the prestige hierarchy rather than to improve error detection.
Abandoning pre-publication review would flood science with error.
Post-publication review already catches most consequential mistakes today.
Retractions are almost always triggered by readers, not by the original reviewers.
A system of rapid preprints combined with structured, public post-publication review would likely detect errors faster than the current peer review model.
Science should treat peer review as an empirical question rather than an article of faith.

theses.text: Peer review at prestige journals is not supported by strong evidence of effectiveness, so acceptance at a prestigious journal carries far less signal about quality than scientists assume.
The current prestige peer review system imposes substantial costs and creates incentives to protect the prestige hierarchy rather than to improve error detection.
A system of rapid preprints combined with structured, public post-publication review would likely detect errors faster than the current prestige peer review model.
Science should treat peer review as an empirical question rather than an article of faith.
